coredata.dc:title: On the role of UV-generated ROS in the desorption of cephalexin from CQDs-based drug-loadable platform
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Khajavi, Zahra
Ghasedi, Arman
Mahdavi, Behnam
Koushki, Ehsan
coredata.dc:description: Since the emergence of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), they have attracted much considerations because of their potential applicability in myriad industries. Innumerable efforts have been made to demystify the mysterious world of CQDs from physicochemical view point. In the present work, CQDs were synthesized via a thermal decomposition method following by post-treatment of the sample using ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) as the surface modifier to prepare EDx-CQDs sample where x represents the weight of the utilized EDTA in the study. Then, cephalexin (Cp) with various concentarations were loaded onto the EDx-CQDs to investigate the possibility of Cp adsorption. Cp desorption process was initiated by a 365 nm UV light source. Noteworthily, structural changes of EDx-CQDs before and after UV exposure of drug-loaded sample were assessed by conducting a series of spectroscopic analyses. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), energy-dispersive x-ray mapping (EDX), ultra-violet-visible (UV–Vis), photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, x-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman scattering spectroscopy, and Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) analyses were employed to scrutinize physical, chemical, and structural alterations. A combined reactive oxygen species (ROS) mechanism was found to be responsible for the desorption of Cp molecules from CQDs surface. The entanglement of both mechanisms initiates a chain ROS reaction leading to complete detachment of Cp molecules.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Adsorption mechanism
Lactame ring cleavage
Hydroxyl radicals
ROS
Electron-hole separation

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of alkali and EDTA treated CQDs
Adsorption and desorption mechanism of Cp

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis procedure of EDTA modified samples (EDx-CQDs) and drug-loaded EDx-CQDs (Cpd-EDx-CQDs).

A) Tem image of ed2-CQDs sample, b) Cp44-ED9-CQDs, c) size distribution of the sample ED9-CQDs with the mean size of 2.3 nm, d) EDX mapping showing the presence of C, N, O, and S atoms in the sample Cp44-ED9-CQDs, and e) EDX curve indicating the weight percentage of each atom.

UV–Vis spectrum of a) EDx-CQDs, b) Cpd –CQDs, c) Cpd-EDx-CQDs samples, d) PL spectra of ED9-CQDs (α spectrum), Cp44-ED9-CQDs before UV irradiation (γ spectrum), and UV exposed Cp44-ED9-CQDs (β spectrum) excited with a 365 nm UV source.
XRD results of a) ED9-CQDs b) Cp44-ED9-CQDs before UV irradiation, and c) UV-exposed Cp44-ED9-CQDs.

Raman spectra of a) Cp44-ED9-CQDs before UV irradiation, and b) UV exposed Cp44-ED9-CQDs fitted with both Gaussian and Voigt functions to extract the most precise results about the structural properties of the samples. The related information of each peak is completely appended in the ESI.

ATR FT-IR spectrum of a) raw Cp, b) ED9-CQDs, c) Cp44-ED9-CQDs samples, d) PL spectra of EDx-CQDs (α spectrum), Cpd-EDx-CQDs before UV irradiation (γ spectrum), and UV exposed Cpd-EDx-CQDs (β spectrum) excited with a 365 nm UV source.

The conceptual illustration of two entangled and possible ROS mechanisms. Mechanism A starts with the ●OH radicals production through UV photolysis of H-bonds formed during adsorption of Cp molecules and ends with the complete detachment of Cp molecules. Mechanism B, which is entangled with the mechanism A, initiates with the cleavage of β-lactame ring following by the production of ●OH radicals and protons through strong electron-hole separation process.

head.ce:title: On the role of UV-generated ROS in the desorption of cephalexin from CQDs-based drug-loadable platform

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Sciences, Hakim Sabzevari University, Sabzevar 96179-76487, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, School of Sciences, Hakim Sabzevari University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, School of Sciences, Hakim Sabzevari University, Sabzevar 96179-76487, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Physics, School of Sciences, Hakim Sabzevari University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Physics, School of Sciences, Hakim Sabzevari University, Sabzevar 96179-76487, Iran.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Z. K. and A. G. contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Since the emergence of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), they have attracted much considerations because of their potential applicability in myriad industries. Innumerable efforts have been made to demystify the mysterious world of CQDs from physicochemical view point. In the present work, CQDs were synthesized via a thermal decomposition method following by post-treatment of the sample using ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) as the surface modifier to prepare EDx-CQDs sample where x represents the weight of the utilized EDTA in the study. Then, cephalexin (Cp) with various concentarations were loaded onto the EDx-CQDs to investigate the possibility of Cp adsorption. Cp desorption process was initiated by a 365 nm UV light source. Noteworthily, structural changes of EDx-CQDs before and after UV exposure of drug-loaded sample were assessed by conducting a series of spectroscopic analyses. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), energy-dispersive x-ray mapping (EDX), ultra-violet-visible (UV–Vis), photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy, x-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman scattering spectroscopy, and Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) analyses were employed to scrutinize physical, chemical, and structural alterations. A combined reactive oxygen species (ROS) mechanism was found to be responsible for the desorption of Cp molecules from CQDs surface. The entanglement of both mechanisms initiates a chain ROS reaction leading to complete detachment of Cp molecules.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are regarded as a new class of fluorophores possessing remarkable optical, chemical and physical characteristics. Numerous advantages of CQDs have turned them into practical and highly applicable materials. The properties, including excellent chemical stability and inertness [1], biocompatibility and biodegradability [2], environment friendly [3], extremely low toxicity and cytotoxicity [4], and photoluminescence (PL) [5] are just some of its merits. Conventionally, CQDs radiate blue emission which encourage researchers to manipulate and tune PL emission of CQDs through various techniques by altering radiative recombination rates [6], changing the size of CQDs (quantum confinement effect) [7], modification of surface states [8], and doping or co-doping of heteroatoms [9] into the crystal structure of CQDs.

Several synthesis method such as microwave-assisted hydrothermal [10], hydro/solvothermal [11,12], laser ablation [13], electrochemical [14], and pyrolysis [15] have been developed to produce CQDs with diversified compositional, structural, and optical characteristics which make them desirable for wide range of applications. Amongst the above-mentioned synthesis routes, pyrolysis approach is being used due to its advantages, such as the preparation of CQDs by using variety of precursors and inexpensive instruments. Synthesizing CQDs from a vast number of precursors such as grass [16], rice husk [17], different species of citrus [18–20], weak acids like citric acid (CA) [21], and banana [22] have attracted numerous considerations due to their beneficial properties. In order to apply some alterations on the surface of CQDs, one of the above-mentioned approaches must be applied to obtain desirable results. Except surface passivation of CQDs, these particles can be doped with various elements such as nitrogen [23], sulfur [24], and boron [25] which are playing a vital role for changing photophysical properties of CQDs by varying band gap energy (Eg).

Blue emissive CQDs are considered the first generation of emissive carbonaceous fluorophores and they have been deeply scrutinized by many researchers. In the past two decades, long-wavelength emissive CQDs like orange and red emissive CQDs have also attracted interests. The size and existence of different functional groups around and/or on the graphene-like planes of CQDs are two major parameters that can alter electronic and photonic characteristics of CQDs [26]. Differentiating the effect of size and functional groups on optical properties of CQDs can be a challenge. This might be due to the fact that the size of CQDs increases by the increment of functional groups [26]. In addition, they can change photonic properties by performing exciton traps leading to non-radiative recombination and decrement of quantum yield. So far, the presence of functional groups has two different aspects; whether they have ability to enhance or rupture the electronic structure of CQDs.

An interesting photophysical property of CQDs is their size-dependent PL property; as the size of the quantum dots (QDs) gets bigger, the emitting PL light shifts towards longer wavelengths [27]. This phenomenon is attributed to the fluctuations of band gap energy and Fermi level alterations which obey an inverse relation which means by reducing band gap energy, emitter sites radiate longer wavelengths [28]. Moreover, the adsorption of other molecules can remarkably change the optoelectronical behaviour of CQDs. For instance, it is established that by the addition of a drug molecule PL emission wavelength of a typical CQDs either can be quenched or shifted towards different wavelengths [29]. This characteristic of CQDs was founded to be promising for the fabrication of PL-switching/quenching drug-loadable platforms [29]. The mechanism which rules over the PL-switching/quenching behavior of CQDs upon adding a substance is attributed to the formation of huge 3D complexes that brings the radiative relaxation processes to a halt [30].

Based on the type of the coordination of drug molecules with CQDs particles causes whether hypsochromic shift or bathochromic [31]. What is more important about a drug nanocarrier, is its detachment efficiency and rate when it delivers the loaded drug to the specified location [31]. Several approaches have been suggested to increase detachment rate efficiencies, including the utilization of non-invasive light sources and ultrasound waves [32]. The former has been attracted many considerations due to its superiorities over other methods, such as being eco-friendly, energy and time efficient, and having the least side effects [33]. The advatangeous characteristics of using light sources for the detachment of loaded drugs at the determined locations, have enabled researchers to develop light-driven nanocarriers with the highest possible accuracy and precision.

Nowadays, CQDs-based drug delivery platforms are being extensively investigated for their potential applicability in theranostics [34], photodynamic therapy [35], chemodynamic therapy [36], for cancer therapy purposes. A considerable number of studies aimed to explore doped-CQDs to acquire the demanded results [36]. In addition, CQDs have been also used for the real-time and immediate detection of antibiotics in a vast range of systems, while their potential for being utilized as nanocarriers for antibiotics has been neglected. Although antibiotics may possess similar chemical structures, their conjugation with other particles may distinctively differ from each other as well as their detachment. Amongst the most importatnt antibiotics, Cephalexin (Cp), as a widely used antibiotic, was employed to be loaded onto the surface of CQDs. To the best of our knowledge, the significance role of ultraviolet (UV) light source on the initiation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) process has not been investigated. In continuation to the previous statement, attachment and detachment procedures of Cp molecules were not correspondingly discussed. A brief comparison between some of the applications of CQDs conjugated with various antibiotics has been provided in Table S1 (Supporting information, SI).

Herein, a facile, simple thermal decomposition synthesis approach was employed to prepare blue emissive CQDs by using citric acid (CA) as the carbon source. The effect of alkali and acidic surface modifications on drug loadability of the samples were investigated. After the purification of the synthesized CQDs samples, they were divided into two groups. The first group of them were funtionalized by ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and for the surface modification of the second group sodium hydroxide (NaOH) was utilized to be used as drug-loadable platforms. EDTA modified CQDs showed promising results in both attachment and detachment of Cp molecules. A UV laser was employed to test the antibiotic detachment. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), photoluminescence (PL) spectrophotometry, UV–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometry, energy-dispersive x-ray (EDS) spectroscopy, x-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, and Raman scattering spectroscopy analyses were conducted to fully scrutinize the structural, chemical, and optical characteristics of the loaded/unloaded samples.

Citric acid (CA, C6H8O7, 99.5%), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA, C10H16N2O8, 99.5%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99.8%), and cephalexin (Cp, C16H17N3O4S, 97%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich company and used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of alkali and EDTA treated CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: A simple thermal decomposition approach was employed to prepare CQDs. Briefly, in a typical procedure, 10 g of CA was poured into a pre-heated Petri dish at 180 ℃. First, CA powder melted and resulted in a clear solution without any color. After 10 min, the color of the solution turned into pale yellow indicating the very first sign of the formation of CQDs [37]. By elapsing another 10 min, the color of the solution turned into orange that is considered a reliable sign of the complete formation of CQDs [37]. The process was continued until the removal of big bubbles to obtain a viscose and glassy solution. Finally, the glassy product possessed burnt orange color was scratched from Petri dish by using a spatula.

An appropriate amount of the prepared CQDs powder was dissolved in a 20 ml of NaOH (60 mg.ml −1) to prepare Na-CQDs solutions. It has been established that NaOH can serve as both surface polisher and stabilizer for CQDs [28] so that large, connected particles can be divided into individual ones with lower size distribution. Four samples with the same quantifications were prepared for further analyses. Four concentrations of EDTA (EDx, x = 0.03, 0.05, 0.07, and 0.09 μg) were tested and it was found out that the ED9 is the optimal concentration to passivize the surface of CQDs. the final product was named ED9-CQDs. A mechanism has been provided in Fig. 1 to illustrate the coordination of Na+ ions around CQDs with ED9-CQDs.

In order to find the optimal concentration of Cpd (d stands for the weight of Cp used in the ED9-CQDs solutions) and CQDs a series of drug loading experiments were conducted. First, Na mediated ED9-CQDs were devided into three shares with the volume of 10 ml for each share. 12, 24, and 44 μg of Cp were added to the solutions and kept stirring on a magnetic strrirer at the room temperature for 24 h to prepare Cp12-, Cp24-, and Cp44-ED9-CQDs respectively. the optimal concentration for Cp was found to be 44 μg in CQDs solution since it had altered the PL properties of ED9-CQDs.

To commence, TEM images of ED9-CQDs and Cp44-ED9-CQDs have been presented in Fig. 2 a and b. Fig. 2a shows distributed individual particles while Cp44-ED9-CQDs particles connect to each other and make densed, connected structures as provided in Fig. 2b. The size distribution (Sd) of the as-synthesized ED9-CQDs has been calculated and plotted in Fig. 2c, which shows a relatively Gaussian Sd with a mean size of 2.3 nm. EDX mapping analysis (Fig. 2c and d) indicated the prominent presence of carbon atoms (71.3 %wt) compared to the other elements. In order to investigate the optical properties of the prepared CQDs samples, UV–Vis analysis was conducted. Fig. 3 represents UV–Vis fingerprint of EDx-CQDs, Na-CQDs, and Cpd-EDx-CQDs samples.

As highlighted in Fig. 3a, three absorption regions named ξ, τ, and υ1 indicate the characteristic absorption of carbogenic core (π-π* transitions), non-bonding transitions related to C-O surface states (n-π* transitions), and C=O (n-π* transitions) respectively [30]. According to the Fig. 3a, ξ region slightly shifted towards bigger wavelengths by increasing the amount of EDTA, from 216 to 224 nm. The reason for this observation might be due to the fact that the increment of EDTA concentration initiates a charge neutrulization process leading to π-π stacking [30]. In τ region, after the addition of EDTA, it vanished as a consequence of π-π stacking and the rupture of C-O functional groups absorption channels. Furthermore, the characteristic peaks in υ2 region experienced a hypsochromic shift similar to the ξ region [30].

Fig. 3b demonstrates UV–Vis spectra of Na-CQDs and its complex with Cp. As seen in Fig. 3b, upon the addition of Cp the characteristic peaks emerged in Na-CQDs, vanished because of the strong coordination between Cp and Na+ ions [38]. It should be noted that in all of the concentrations of Cp, the aggregation process was initiated and sediments contained Na cations and Cp molecules were precipitated at the botttom of the beaker. Therefore, due to the failure in the functionalization process of CQDs using Na and Cp, the optimum concentration of EDTA (ED9-CQDs sample) was first stabilized with Na solution and then different concentrations of Cp were added to the Na mediated ED9-CQDs solution.

Fig. 3c represents UV–Vis spectrum of the as-prepared samples. Both characteristic regions have been named χ, and ψ that are ascribed to π-π* and n-π* transitions, respectively [30]. It can be seen that after the addition of Cp to the alkali mediated ED9-CQDs the peak intensity related to π-π* transition first reduced and shifted towards lower wavelengths from 224 nm for ED9-CQDs to 206 nm for Cp12-ED9-CQDs. Then, the peak experienced a bathochromic shift (red shift) by increasing the concentration of Cp from 206 nm for Cp12-ED9-CQDs to 210 nm for Cp44-ED9-CQDs. It has been established that the bathochromic shift of π-π* transition happens as a consequence of increasing the lateral size through the formation of J-aggregates, alteration of charge accumulation at the interface of the particles and ligands, and increment of the emissive surface states [30].

Here the red-shift of the π-π* characteristics can be attributed to two reasons; the first one is the increment of the lateral size of the particles, and the second one is the alteration of charge transfer pathways through coordination of Cp molecules with the surface of the alkali-EDTA modified CQDs. Considering the peaks in ψ region, no detectable shift can be observed. But, the intensity of the peaks constantly reduced by the increment of Cp concentration which means instant formation of edge bonds between Cp molecules and ED9-CQDs particles mostly through hydrogen bonds [39]. The complementary detail of each peak has been tabulated in Table S2.

The complete adsorption of Cp on the surface of ED9-CQDs particles encouraged us to investigate detachment of Cp molecules using a UV light source. In order to scrutinize the possible mechanism(s) for the desorption of Cp, structural charactetistics of Cp44-ED9-CQDs were examined before and after UV irradiation. Fig. 3d exhibits three PL spectra entitled α, β, and γ spectra that each of them represents PL behaviour of ED9-CQDs, UV exposed Cp44-ED9-CQDs, and Cp44-ED9-CQDs, respectively. It is worth mentioning that all of the PL analyses have been conducted by employing a 365 nm excitation source.

α spectrum shows ED9-CQDs PL emision possessing a maximum emission at 473 nm. After the addition of Cp, PL emission of the final product shifted towards bigger wavelengths with a maximum at 584 nm which is assigned as γ spectrum. Also, a band-tail at 700 nm has appeared that had relatively negligible contribution to the emission. New relaxation channels will be evidently formed due to the clustering [30]; therefore, contribution of new emissions after the addition of Cp to PL emision is not an unexpected issue.

After 35 min of exposing Cp44-ED9-CQDs to a 365 nm UV light source, the PL emission of the sample was recorded (β spectrum). PL emission of the sample reduce to 538 nm as the consequence of Cp detachment from the surface of ED9-CQDs particles through reactive oxygen sepcies (ROS) proccess which will be thoroughly discussed in the next section. The band-tail emerged in the γ spectrum, notably contributed to the emission which is consistent with given explanations earlier about the formation of new relaxation channels upon clustering [30].

Beneficial information can be inferred from band gap (Eg) energy calculations (see Fig. S1 a-k, and Table S3 in the SI for a complete information list about the Eg calculations) which help us to gain deeper insights from the attachment process of Cp molecules and optical behaviour of conjugated CQDs samples. According to the results shown in Table S3, Eg values for EDx-CQDs samples hovered around ∼ 4.7 eV indicating feeble influence of EDTA on CQDs electronic behaviour. By the addition of Na cationic solution, Eg values for this sample notably changed and Eg reduced to 3.21 eV which is related to the edge energy state alterations [30]. Moreover, the sample showed a multigap nature, as a consequence of surface polishing of CQDs using Na ions [30]. Following this, another Eg was emerged with the approximate energy of 3.87 eV which can be attributed to the surface defects [30]. By the addition of Cp into the CQDs sample Eg experienced relatively huge increment and reached to 4.95 eV and lingered around this value despite increasing Cp concentration. Conjugation of CQDs with both Cp and EDTA exhibited similar behaviour with an Eg of about 5.12 eV. These observations enabled us to find a consistency between the obtained results and experimental observations.

Increasing Cp concentration create an electrical potential around CQDs which confines moving electrons and leads to more localization of the electrons in the structure. This imposes more constraning force on the vast majority of the electrons that can be excited with lower energies and in this case, the electrons with higher energies inevitably contribute to the absorption process. Apart form the statements expressed earlier, increasing Cp concentration in ED9-CQDs sample provide a favorable condition for Cp molecules to make more bonds with free reactive chemical sites that results in the complete elimination of absorption sites, which could be excited with lower energies, and the sample experienced an increase in Eg. In such cases intersystem crossing (ISC) and internal conversions (IC) are highly likely to happen. That is to say, although increasing Cp concentration quenches excitation channels, it leads to the initiation of huge number of ISC and IC processes. Fig. 3c clearly shows an enhancement in the band tail which emerged around 700 nm as a result of ISC/IC processes. When Cp44-ED9-CQDs sample was exposed to a 365 nm UV light source (β spectrum), Cp molecules detached from the surface of CQDs and created radiative relaxation pathways emitting in the aforementioned region. Another issue which is worth mentioning is that the increment of Eg creates a benign condition to initiate more powerful electron-hole recombination leading to ROS.

XRD analysis was conducted to explore possible staructural alterations of unexposed and UV exposed Cp44-ED9-CQDs samples. Fig. 4 a represents XRD pattern of ED9-CQDs sample which possesses a broad peak at 24.67° with the full-width at half maximum (FWHM) of 12.91 related to (002) crystal plane that can be matched with the JCPDS card no. 26-1076. As seen in Fig. 4b, Cp44-ED9-CQDs showed one characteristic peak at 24.92° with the FWHM of 16.95 and a very broad peak at 43.62° which are related to (002) (JCPDS card no. 26-1076) and (100) (JCPDS card no. 75-0444) planes [40], respectively. Fig. 4c represents XRD results of Cp44-ED9-CQDs after the irradiation using a UV light source. The first peak shifted towards bigger degrees following by the increment of FWHM from 16.95 to 19.27 that depict changes in both crystalline structure and size distribution of the particles. The shift of the peak from 24.92° to 26.22° indicates the contraction of interplanar distance of the crystal. In addition, FWHM of the sample was simultaneously reduced which is in excellent consistency with the results from PL and UV spectra.

Interplanar spacing (d-spacing) was calculated by utilizing Bragg’s formula as below:
(1)
d = λ / 2 s i n θ
where d, λ, and θ are d-spacing, x-ray beam wavelength (0.1541 nm), and diffraction angle, respectively. As embedded in the Fig. 4, ED9-CQDs has a d-spacing of 0.36 nm which is almost similar to the other literature [39]. The addition of Cp exerts a feeble influence on the d-spacing of (002) plane as it is indicated in Fig. 4a and b. Nonetheless, it created a constraining force around CQDs and improved the rigidity of the particles by enhancing (100) plane that has a d-spacing of 0.21 nm. As shown in Fig. 4c, the UV-exposed sample d-spacing reduced to 0.28 nm. In order to justifiably interpret this reduction, three simultaneous possibilities namely dehydration, drug detachment, and delamination of graphene-like planes have to be considered that will be scrutinized through future works.

To achieve a deeper insight into the structural changes of UV exposed Cp44-ED9-CQDs, Raman analysis was conducted and its results are presented in Fig. 5 . In recent years, Raman has been became a versatile tool to scrutinize structural characteristics of carbon-based quantum materials. In the meantime, deconvolution of the Raman peaks has enabled researchers to extract more information about the changes in crystalline structure. Raman fingerprint of the carbon-based materials is usually made of two vibrations known as Dband and Gband, that represent disordered (defects) and graphitized (crystalline) regions. It should be noted that Dband is ascribed to zig-zag and armchair states and Gband is related to C=C bonds. The other sub-bands, including D1, D2, D3, and D’ are attributed to sp2-sp3 carbon domains, COOH/C–OH, C=O/C–O, and crystalline defects respectively [30].

Various models have been introduced to deconvolute a Raman spectra and amongst them the six-peak model has been established as a reliable deconvolution method. In the present work, a six-peak model with the combination of Gaussian and Voigt functions has been utilized to deconvolute Raman bands. As indicated in Fig. 5a, Raman spectrum of Cp44-ED9-CQDs sample include six peaks while the UV exposed sample consists of five sub-bands (Fig. 5b). By comparing the results it can be realized that the D1 peak is absent when the sample was irradiated by a UV light source. This is due to the elimination of C-H bonds related to sp3 hybridization during the irradiation process. Considering the D2 peak, its intensity reduced after exposing the sample to the UV light sourcce. It implies that during the UV treatment of the sample, a relatively extensive deprotonation has been initiated [30].

In pace with the D peak, its position almost remains constant after exposing the sample to UV light. Since the D peak shows the of graphene-like sheet edge type its, incremental and decremental trend of the peak intensity has been assigned to the conversion of the edge type [39]. According to the reason given earlier, the peak intensity increases by 2.5-fold in comparison with the untreated sample implied the conversion of armchair edge state to zig-zag ones [30]. Regarding the D3 peak, which is corresponding for the carbonyl surface functional groups C=O/C–O, its intensity has strengthened after UV irradiaton as the consequence of deprotonation of Cp44-ED9-CQDs sample.

Intriguingly, the G peak strongly enhanced compared with the unexposed Cp44-ED9-CQDs sample because of eliminating sp3 modes during UV exposing process [41]. Moreover, the reduction of the G peak position from 1441 to 1422 cm−1 indicates the incerement of the bond disorders. Finally, the information extracted from the D’ peak position and intensity can be used to interpret the evolution and creation of crystalline defects such as pentagonal/heptagonal defects as well as vacancies. The irradiated sample exhibited the D’ peak with 2-fold higher intensity than that of unexposed sample. This demonstrates the presence of higher density of defects in the UV-exposed Cp44-ED9-CQDs sample. The details of the deconvoluted peaks have been provided in Table S4 in the SI.

To track changes of the functional groups during adsorption and desorption of Cp from ED9-CQDs surface a series of ATR-FTIR analyses ought to be conducted. Attenuated total reflectance FT-IR (ATR-FTIR) analysis was employed to further investigate both UV-irradiated and unexposed drug-loaded samples. The FT-IR spectrum of raw Cp is shown in Fig. 6 a. According to the results, the peak at 3325 cm−1 belongs to the overlapping stretching vibration of the O-H/N-H bond. The bands at 2975 and 2882 cm−1 are attributed to symmetric and asymmetric aliphatic C-H vibrations. The peaks in regions 1380 to 1755 cm−1 belong to C=C and C=O bonds. Furthermore, the peak at ∼ 1275 cm−1 is attributed to the –C=ONH vibrations [42]. The peaks at 1046 cm−1 attributes to anhydride stretching vibration of O-H and 1089 cm−1 belong to the C–O bond. In addition, the peak at 880 cm−1 is assigned to the wagging vibration of C–N related to secondary amine groups [43]. The presence of a broad peak at 615 cm−1 belongs to the C–H on the edge of the π-ring.

Fig. 6b, shows FT-IR spectrum of ED9-CQDs sample. it clearly demonstrates the dominance of O-H/N-H stretching vibrations at ∼ 3300 cm−1, C=N/C=O at ∼ 1630 cm−1, and C–H vibrations situated on the edges at ∼ 615 cm−1. Fig. 6c exhibits the spectrum of interest here in this work and belongs to Cp44-ED9-CQDs before irradiation. It can be observed that the adsorption of Cp on the surface of ED9-CQDs partciles was mainly done through H-bonding based on emerged peak at ∼ 1570 cm−1 related to N-H vibrations. Additionally, the peak which is belong to –C=ONH remained unchanged with a feeble intensity enhancement. The other peaks showed similar behaviour to the other samples. Lastly, the drug-loaded sample was exposed to a UV light source to investigate the possibility of Cp detachment by a UV source. As observed in Fig. 6d, after exposing the sample to the UV radiation, the characteristic peaks assigned to N-H, C-H, and –C=ONH vibrations were vanished. Also, the latter was changed to COO– as the consequence of heavy deprotonation of the surface functional groups during ROS process. Fig. S2 exhibits FT-IR spectra for all of the samples in one graph.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption and desorption mechanism of Cp
ce:section.ce:para: According to the interpretations provided in the previous section, the following mechanism can be suggested to scientifically justify adsorption/desorption mechanism happened before/after UV irradiation. Due to the fact that CQDs are promising “ROS-generators” [44], they can be utilized as ●OH radical producers. Two possible desorption mechanisms have been suggested as follows that were happened through ROS process. By exposing ED9-CQDs sample to a UV source, photoexcited charge carriers, which are electrons in this case, create holes on the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) because of their transition to an excited state. On the one hand, the photoexcited charge carriers contribute to producing ●OH radicals in the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). On the other hand, remained highly reactive holes in the HOMO reacting with water molecules to produce ●OH radicals, too. ●OH radicals are produced from breaking of H-bonds which were created during the adsorption of Cp on the surface of ED9-CQDs (mechanism A).

Apart from the mechanism A expressed above, another possible mechanism is the production of ●OH radicals from the cleavage of β-lactame ring during UV irradiation. This process also leads to the production of excessive amount of H+ [38]. Therefore, produced ●OH radicals and protons (H+) initiates the formation process of water molecules. The process results in inhomogenous declustering of Cp44-ED9-CQDs monoliths (mechanism B). It can be said that both mechanisms can be initiated simultaneously. In other words, from a physicochemical viewpoint, the presented mechanisms are entangled to each other and require one another like a chain reaction process. A conceptual illustration of the explanations given earlier has been provided in Fig. 7 .

To conclude, EDTA-modified CQDs samples have been prepared via a facile, one step thermal decomposition synthesis approach. It was found out that the optimum concentration of EDTA for modifying the surface of CQDs is 0.09 μg to prepare the most efficient CQDs-based drug-loadable platform. Having loaded the ED9-CQDs with cephalexin, both PL and UV–Vis spectra of the samples altered. So, a UV light source was used to explore desorption possibility of Cp from the ED9-CQDs sample. The PL spectra results suggested the formation of clusters during drug loading process and starting ROS process during UV irradiation. Structural changes of the samples were further investigated by employing Raman spectroscopy. By deconvoluting Raman spectra, it was realized that an extensive deprotonation process, which stems from ROS process, has been initiated during UV irradiation. ATR-FTIR experiment was conducted to prove the observations in Raman spectroscopy. Elimination of H-bonds especially the ones related to amine groups established the hypothesis given for the initiation of ROS process. Lastly, a photo-physico-chemical mechanism was provided to justify the explanations given in this paper that can be utilized for further investigations. The present study has brightened one of the potentials of CQDs and requires to be investigated in real situation by using gram-positive and gram-negative bacteries.
The data can be provided from the authors upon reasonable request.

Zahra Khajavi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Arman Ghasedi: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Behnam Mahdavi: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Ehsan Koushki: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Validation, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.114813.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
